does: This paper identifies and formalizes a structured set of concrete research problems that arise when deploying machine learning systems in real-world environments. It catalogs safety challenges including reward misspecification, where systems optimize proxies that diverge from intended objectives; safe exploration, where agents must learn without causing unacceptable harms; robustness to distributional shift and adversarial inputs; scalable oversight of increasingly capable systems; and safe interruptibility. Rather than treating AI safety as an abstract philosophical concern, the work grounds these issues in practical scenarios—autonomous vehicles, recommender systems, robotic control—and articulates them as technical problems amenable to empirical research.
connects: This work builds on growing recognition within the machine learning community that safety cannot be bolted on after deployment but must be integrated into system design from the start. It responds to limitations in both classical AI safety literature, which often lacked empirical grounding, and in mainstream ML research, which had largely neglected safety considerations. The paper synthesizes concerns raised across reinforcement learning, robotics, and systems deployment, while engaging with theoretical work on specification gaming and value alignment from AI safety pioneers. It positions safety not as separate from performance but as a fundamental design constraint alongside accuracy and efficiency.
enables: This taxonomy of concrete problems establishes a research agenda that makes AI safety accessible to mainstream ML researchers and practitioners. It creates a common vocabulary and problem formulation that facilitates collaboration across academic and industry labs. By decomposing safety into specific technical challenges with measurable aspects, the paper enables targeted research programs: reward learning from human feedback, adversarial robustness methods, uncertainty quantification, and interpretability techniques can now be motivated as safety tools. This framework has subsequently enabled funding bodies and conferences to organize safety research coherently and motivated the development of safety-focused benchmarks and evaluation protocols across the ML community.
